title: First rocket launch!
description: Print and launch your own model rocket. Follow the steps to print, assemble, and launch the rocket. Get ready for liftoff!

dialogue.text: Nova here—rocketry lead. I work with Sydney and Phoenix, and today we're launching your first model rocket. Ready to light the fuse?

options.text: You betcha! How, uh, how high is this rocket thing launching?

dialogue.text: I've flown this exact design. Expect about 35 meters in calm air, but wind and air density can swing that number.

options.text: Oh, cool, that doesn't sound very high, but I'm sure I'm underestimating what that looks like! And I don't need some special paperwork from the government to launch this thing?

dialogue.text: We're clear under FAA hobby limits: under 1500 grams total and under 125 g of propellant. We're around 90 g for the body and 18 g for the motor. Find a wide, open launch area and we're good.

options.text: Gotcha, that quiets my fears a bit. So, what do I need to do to get this thing ready to launch?

dialogue.text: First step: 3D print the airframe. Lightweight PLA, about an hour on the starter printer.

options.text: Print the rocket?? Aren't there, like, pre-made rockets I can buy?

dialogue.text: You could buy one, but printing teaches the airframe. Use the printer Sydney gave you. Four parts, then glue and assembly. This is MVP rocketry—upgrades come after we stick the landing.

options.text: Works for me! What do I need to print?

dialogue.text: Modularity saves time and filament. Print a nosecone, body tube, fincan, and a nosecone coupler that joins the nosecone to the body tube. One printer means one part at a time, unless you scale up. I'm in no rush.

options.text: Fin! Well, not yet, but soon!
A couple of these should do the trick! Get it? A couple? Nosecone coupler? Hah! Actually I only need the one ...
Alright, all 4 components are now printed! What's next?

dialogue.text: Assembly time. You'll need a solid rocket motor, superglue, and a Kevlar shock cord. Grab them, then use this schematic to assemble the airframe.

options.text: The rocket's assembled! Are we ready for launch?

dialogue.text: Almost there. You'll need a launch pad, igniter, and launch controller. The pad keeps the trajectory straight; the controller fires the motor. I'll loan you spares.

options.text: Oh cool, I'll never turn down free stuff!
We are go for launch!!! Right?

dialogue.text: We are go. Place the rocket on the pad, insert the igniter, connect to the controller, and back up to a safe distance. When you're ready, press launch and watch it fly.

options.text: Whoa! I can't believe I launched a rocket!! It didn't survive the landing, though, unfortunately.

dialogue.text: That was a success, even without recovery. You're officially a rocketeer. Take this token—and next iteration, we'll tune for reuse. For now, enjoy your first launch.

options.text: Houston, we do not have a problem!
